title: Latin: The Medieval Superstar
text: Latin overwhelmingly dominates early manuscript production, with a very high probability of use in both religious and non-religious texts during the 1100s and 1200s. However, its dominance gradually declines over time, especially after the 1400s, reflecting the increasing use of vernacular languages.

title: Ancient Greek: Rare but Persistent
text: Ancient Greek appears only in a small number of manuscripts in this dataset. Its presence remains relatively low throughout the period shown in the graph, especially when compared with languages such as Latin or English. This reflects the fact that Greek texts circulated less widely in Western European manuscript culture, although some continued to be copied and studied, particularly in scholarly or religious contexts.

title: Middle English on the Rise
text: Middle English begins to appear more frequently from the 1300s onward. Its growing presence reflects the increasing use of vernacular language in manuscripts, particularly for practical, literary, and everyday texts.

title: Italian Finds Its Place
text: Italian begins to appear more frequently in the later centuries of the period shown in the graph, especially from the 1400s onward. Although it never becomes as common as Latin, its presence reflects the growing use of vernacular languages in manuscript culture, particularly in literary, scientific, and practical texts.

title: Anglo-Norman in Medieval England
text: Anglo-Norman shows its strongest presence in the 1200s and 1300s, reflecting its importance in England after the Norman Conquest. Its use declines in later centuries as English gradually becomes more prominent in manuscript production.

title: Middle French: A Quiet Presence
text: Middle French appears regularly in the manuscripts across the period shown in the graph. Although it is less common than Latin, it is clearly present in the collection and reflects the growing use of French as a written vernacular. The pattern in the graph may also reflect the particular manuscripts preserved in the library, rather than the full range of texts produced in the medieval period.

title: English Steps into the Spotlight
text: English becomes increasingly common from the 1400s onward, especially in non-religious manuscripts. This trend reflects the growing importance of vernacular writing for practical, literary, and administrative purposes.

title: Old French: Early Vernacular Writing
text: Old French also appears most frequently in the 1200s and 1300s. After this early peak, its presence declines, while English and other vernacular languages become increasingly common in manuscripts.
